extra.reasoning:
The safety property requires globally(opted_out implies not sent): in every reachable state where opted_out is true, sent must be false.

Transition t5 directly contradicts this: [t5] on send_attempt if (opted_out and ticks >= 2): sent := true. This sets sent := true precisely while opted_out is true, so any reachable firing of t5 violates the invariant.

Reachable counterexample (4 events, within the 6-event bound):
1. optout -> t1 fires (guard true): opted_out=true, sent=false, ticks=0.
2. tick -> t4 fires: ticks=1, sent=false, opted_out=true.
3. tick -> t4 fires: ticks=2, sent=false, opted_out=true.
4. send_attempt -> now opted_out=true and ticks>=2, so both t3 (guard: opted_out) and t5 (guard: opted_out and ticks>=2) are enabled. No priority ordering is specified between matching transitions, so the system is nondeterministic and t5 is a legal choice. Firing t5 yields opted_out=true, sent=true, ticks=2.

This final state has opted_out=true and sent=true, violating opted_out implies not sent. Because safety verification requires the property to hold on ALL reachable executions, the existence of this one violating execution (via t5) means the property is violated. Note t2 (send when not opted_out) and t3 (send when opted_out -> sent:=false) are consistent with the property; t5 is the offending rule whose guard is reachable.
